conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions. Let me start by gathering some basic information.

Do you have any children that you're responsible for?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I do. I have one child - my son Alex. He's 8 years old and lives at home with me. I'd like to know if I can claim Child Benefit for him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! That's helpful to know about Alex.

Before I can assess your eligibility, let me read the official guidance to ensure I give you accurate information.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for waiting. Now I have the official guidance, I need to ask you a few questions about your situation to determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

First, do you currently live in the UK?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you. 

You mentioned that Alex lives at home with you. Can you confirm that you are responsible for him - meaning you're the one who provides for his day-to-day needs like food, clothes, and general care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, absolutely. I'm responsible for him - I provide everything he needs, his food, clothes, and I take care of him.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect, that's very helpful.

Just a few more questions to complete the assessment. Is Alex currently in hospital, in care, or in any residential accommodation arranged by your local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Actually, yes - Alex has been in hospital for a while now. It's been a few months.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
I'm sorry to hear that Alex has been in hospital. I need to understand this situation a bit more to assess your eligibility accurately.

Could you tell me approximately how many weeks Alex has been in hospital?

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I understand this must be a difficult time for you.

Since Alex has been in hospital for more than 12 weeks, I need to ask: do you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital? This could include things like clothes, toys, treats, or other items for him.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I do. I still buy things for him - clothes, magazines, his favorite snacks and treats. I make sure he has what he needs while he's there.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. That's very helpful and shows you're continuing to support Alex during his hospital stay.

Just one final question: is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Alex, or has anyone else told you they intend to claim for him?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for him. It's just me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Good news! **You are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.**

Here's why:

✓ Alex is 8 years old (under 16), so he meets the age requirement  
✓ You live in the UK and are responsible for Alex's care  
✓ While Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (which is more than the usual 12-week limit), there's an important exception: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them  
✓ You've confirmed that you regularly buy things for Alex like clothes, magazines, snacks and treats while he's in hospital - this qualifies for the exception  
✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

Because you're continuing to support Alex financially during his hospital stay, you remain eligible for Child Benefit.

If you haven't already claimed, you can apply for Child Benefit. If you're already receiving it, your payments should continue as long as you keep regularly spending money on Alex during his hospital stay.

I hope Alex recovers well. If you have any other questions, please feel free to ask.

claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child in hospital for 15 weeks but claimant regularly spends on child
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old, which is under 16, so he meets the age requirement for Child Benefit. You live in the UK (Manchester) and are responsible for Alex, providing for his day-to-day needs. Although Alex has been in hospital for 15 weeks (which exceeds the 12-week limit that would normally affect Child Benefit), there is an exception to this rule: you can continue to receive Child Benefit for a child in a UK hospital if you regularly spend money on them. You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital, including buying him clothes, magazines, snacks and treats. This meets the exception criteria. Additionally, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and is responsible for their 8-year-old son Alex, who lives with them but is currently in hospital (15 weeks). While normally Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital, an exception applies because the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during his hospital stay. No one else is claiming for Alex. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.

You have confirmed that you regularly spend money on Alex while he's in hospital, including buying him clothes, magazines, snacks and treats. This meets the exception criteria. Additionally, no one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. Therefore, you are eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

While normally Child Benefit stops after 12 weeks in hospital, an exception applies because the claimant regularly spends money on Alex during his hospital stay. No one else is claiming for Alex. The claimant meets all eligibility criteria for Child Benefit.
